content: I'm struggling with adjusting my routine after this knee injury. I used to do Brazilian jiu-jitsu three times a week and now I'm mostly stuck at home.
That sounds really frustrating. How long have you been recovering, and what does your doctor say about returning to BJJ?
It's been about six weeks since my meniscus surgery. The doctor says I can start light activity in another month, but no sparring for at least three more months.
That's a long timeline. Have you found any alternative activities that keep you engaged while you heal?
I've been doing a lot of speedcubing - you know, those Rubik's cube puzzles. I can solve a 3x3 in about 45 seconds now.
That's impressive! Speedcubing requires great finger dexterity and mental focus. It's a good substitute for the physical challenge of BJJ.
Yeah, it keeps my mind sharp. My daughter Lily has gotten into it too - she's only 10 but already down to about 2 minutes on her solves.
That's wonderful family bonding time! Do you practice together or compete against each other?
We have mini competitions at home. I let her win sometimes, but she's catching up fast. My wife thinks it's cute how competitive we get.
Family activities that bring out everyone's playful side are the best. How has the injury affected your work at the healthcare company?
I'm a QA lead there, so I can mostly work from home which has been good. We're testing a new patient management system right now - lots of crossword puzzles to keep my brain active during meetings.
Crosswords during meetings? That's multitasking! Does your team know about your recovery process?
Yeah, they've been really supportive. We actually started a podcast club at work - we listen to different health tech podcasts and discuss them weekly.
That's a creative team-building activity! What kind of podcasts have you been enjoying lately?
Mostly ones about medical software innovations and patient data security. It ties into our work but also helps me stay current in the field while I'm healing.
